Coordination is the process through which two or more organs interact and complement the functions of one another. For example, when we do physical exercises,... full_notes:
Body Fluids and Circulation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 230 BIOLOGY As you know, the functions of the organs/organ systems in our body must be coordinated to maintain homeostasis.

The text explains that coordination is the process through which two or more organs interact and complement the functions of one another.

The text explains that for example, when we do physical exercises, the energy demand is increased for maintaining an increased muscular activity.

The text explains that the increased supply of oxygen necessitates an increase in the rate of respiration, heart beat and increased blood flow via blood vessels.

The text explains that when physical exercise is stopped, the activities of nerves, lungs, heart and kidney gradually return to their normal conditions.

The text explains that thus, the functions of muscles, lungs, heart, blood vessels, kidney and other organs are coordinated while performing physical exercises.

The text explains that in our body the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion.

The text explains that the neural system provides an organised network of point-to-point connections for a quick coordination.

The text explains that the endocrine system provides chemical integration through hormones.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will learn about the neural system of human, mechanisms of neural coordination like transmission of nerve impulse and impulse conduction across a synapse.

The text explains that nEURAL CONTROL AND COORDINATION CHAPTER 18 18.1 Neural System 18.2 Human Neural System 18.3 Neuron as Structural and Functional Unit of Neural System 18.4 Central Neural System

The text explains that nEURAL CONTROL AND COORDINATION 231 18.1 NEURAL SYSTEM The neural system of all animals is composed of highly specialised cells called neurons which can detect, receive and transmit different kinds of stimuli.

The text explains that the neural organisation is very simple in lower invertebrates.

The text explains that for example, in Hydra it is composed of a network of neurons.

The text explains that the neural system is better organised in insects, where a brain is present along with a number of ganglia and neural tissues.

The text explains that the vertebrates have a more developed neural system.

The text explains that 18.2 HUMAN NEURAL SYSTEM The human neural system is divided into two parts : (i) the central neural system (CNS) (ii) the peripheral neural system (PNS) The CNS includes the brain and the spinal cord and is the site of information processing and control.

The text explains that the PNS is divided into two divisions called somatic neural system and autonomic neural system.

The text explains that the somatic neural system relays impulses from the CNS to skeletal muscles while the autonomic neural system transmits impulses from the CNS to the involuntary organs and smooth muscles of the body.

The text explains that the autonomic neural system is further classified into sympathetic neural system and parasympathetic neural system.

The text explains that visceral nervous system is the part of the peripheral nervous system that comprises the whole complex of nerves, fibres, ganglia, and plexuses by which impulses travel from the central nervous system to the viscera and from the viscera to the central nervous system.

The text explains that 18.3 NEURON AS STRUCTURAL AND FUNCTIONAL UNIT OF NEURAL SYSTEM A neuron is a microscopic structure composed of three major parts, namely, cell body, dendrites and axon (Figure 18.1).

The text explains that each branch terminates as a bulb-like structure called synaptic knob which possess synaptic vesicles containing chemicals called neurotransmitters.

The text explains that unmyelinated nerve fibre is enclosed by a Schwann cell that does not form a myelin sheath around the axon, and is commonly found in autonomous and the somatic neural systems.

The text explains that as a result, the outer surface of the axonal membrane possesses a positive charge while its inner surface Figure 18.1 Structure of a neuron

The text explains that electrical current can flow directly from one neuron into the other across these synapses.

The text explains that 18.4 CENTRAL NEURAL SYSTEM The brain is the central information processing organ of our body, and acts as the ‘command and control system’.

The text explains that it is also the site for processing of vision, hearing, speech, memory, intelligence, emotions and thoughts.

The text explains that a deep cleft divides the cerebrum longitudinally into two halves, which are termed as the left and right cerebral hemispheres.

The text explains that the cerebrum wraps around a structure called thalamus, which is a major coordinating centre for sensory and motor signaling.

The text explains that the hypothalamus contains a number of centres which control body temperature, urge for eating and drinking.

The text explains that the inner parts of cerebral hemispheres and a group of associated deep structures like amygdala, hippocampus, etc., form a complex structure called the limbic lobe or limbic system.

The text explains that nEURAL CONTROL AND COORDINATION 237 SUMMARY The neural system coordinates and integrates functions as well as metabolic and homeostatic activities of all the organs.

The text explains that neurons, the functional units of neural system are excitable cells due to a differential concentration gradient of ions across the membrane.

The text explains that the nerve impulse is conducted along the axon membrane in the form of a wave of depolarisation and repolarisation.

The text explains that human neural system consists of two parts : (i) central neural system (CNS) and (ii) the peripheral neural system.

The text explains that a very important part of the forebrain called hypothalamus controls the body temperature, eating and drinking.

The text explains that inner parts of cerebral hemispheres and a group of associated deep structures form a complex structure called limbic system which is concerned with olfaction, autonomic responses, regulation of sexual behaviour, expression of emotional reactions, and motivation.

The text explains that the cerebellum integrates information received from the semicircular canals of the ear and the auditory system.

The text explains that briefly describe the structure of the Brain 2.

The text explains that compare the following: (a) Central neural system (CNS) and Peripheral neural system (PNS) (b) Resting potential and action potential 3.

The text explains that explain the following processes: (a) Polarisation of the membrane of a nerve fibre (b) Depolarisation of the membrane of a nerve fibre (c) Transmission of a nerve impulse across a chemical synapse

The text explains that draw labelled diagrams of the following: (a) Neuron (b) Brain 5.

The text explains that write short notes on the following: (a) Neural coordination (b) Forebrain (c) Midbrain (d) Hindbrain (e) Synapse 6.

The text explains that give a brief account of Mechanism of synaptic transmission.

The text explains that explain the role of Na+ in the generation of action potential.

The text explains that differentiate between: (a) Myelinated and non-myelinated axons (b) Dendrites and axons (c) Thalamus and Hypothalamus (d) Cerebrum and Cerebellum 9.

The text explains that (b) Which part of our central neural system acts as a master clock?

The text explains that distinguish between: (a) afferent neurons and efferent neurons (b) impulse conduction in a myelinated nerve fibre and unmyelinated nerve fibre (f) cranial nerves and spinal nerves.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18: Body Fluids and Circulation

concise_notes.definition: 230 BIOLOGY As you know, the functions of the organs/organ systems in our body must be coordinated to maintain homeostasis.
In our body the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion.
Coordination is the process through which two or more organs interact and complement the functions of one another.
In this chapter, you will learn about the neural system of human, mechanisms of neural coordination like transmission of nerve impulse and impulse conduction across a synapse.
In our body the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion.
In this chapter, you will learn about the neural system of human, mechanisms of neural coordination like transmission of nerve impulse and impulse conduction across a synapse.
NEURAL CONTROL AND COORDINATION CHAPTER 18 18.1 Neural System 18.2 Human Neural System 18.3 Neuron as Structural and Functional Unit of Neural System 18.4 Central Neural System
NEURAL CONTROL AND COORDINATION CHAPTER 18 18.1 Neural System 18.2 Human Neural System 18.3 Neuron as Structural and Functional Unit of Neural System 18.4 Central Neural System
In our body the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all the activities of the organs so that they function in a synchronised fashion.
In this chapter, you will learn about the neural system of human, mechanisms of neural coordination like transmission of nerve impulse and impulse conduction across a synapse.
